title: @cyrilXBT: https://t.co/bsV5kJcWDO
rawContent:
Tweet by @cyrilXBT (CyrilXBT)
Author bio: AI • Tech • Crypto | Sharing what I see before everyone else does.
Text: https://t.co/bsV5kJcWDO
Created: 2026-06-14T02:26:37.000Z
Metrics: {"retweet_count":18,"reply_count":12,"like_count":122,"quote_count":1,"bookmark_count":141,"impression_count":11714}
Article content: {"preview_text":"Most people use Claude Fable 5 the same way they used every previous model.
They open a chat. They type a question. They get an answer. They close the tab.
That approach with Fable 5 is like using a","title":"300 Claude Fable 5 Prompts That Replace Hours of Manual Work Every Single Day","entities":{"mentions":[{"start":7,"end":16,"username":"cyrilXBT"}]},"plain_text":"Most people use Claude Fable 5 the same way they used every previous model.
They open a chat. They type a question. They get an answer. They close the tab.
That approach with Fable 5 is like using a Formula 1 car to drive to the grocery store.
Fable 5 is a Mythos-class model designed for long-running, complex, multi-step work that previous models could not handle reliably. It has a 1 million token context window. It can operate autonomously for days. It admits when it fails. It reasons at a depth that changes what is possible in a single session.
The prompts in this article are built for that capability level.
Not generic prompts that work on any model. Prompts engineered for what Fable 5 specifically can do that nothing before it could do as reliably.
Organized into ten categories. Thirty prompts per category. Three hundred total.
Category 1: Deep Research and Synthesis (30 Prompts)
These prompts leverage Fable 5's ability to hold massive context and reason across it simultaneously.
1. Read everything I've given you and produce a synthesis 
that identifies the three claims all sources agree on, 
the three claims they contradict each other on, and the 
single most important thing none of them address.

2. You are a senior research analyst. Read this corpus 
and tell me what a smart person who read all of it 
would believe that someone who read only the headlines 
would miss entirely.

3. I'm researching [TOPIC]. Read these sources and 
tell me: what does the evidence clearly support, what 
is genuinely contested, and what questions would I 
need to answer to resolve the contested parts?

4. Read this research and steelman the position I 
disagree with most strongly. Give me the best version 
of the argument against my current position using 
only the evidence provided.

5. Treat this collection of documents as a dataset. 
What patterns appear across them that would be 
invisible reading any individual document alone?

6. Read everything and identify the assumption that, 
if false, would invalidate the most conclusions. 
Explain exactly why that assumption is load-bearing.

7. I need to brief someone who has 10 minutes. 
Read this entire corpus and give me the five things 
they absolutely cannot afford not to know. Nothing else.

8. Read these sources and map the disagreements. 
Not surface disagreements. The underlying 
disagreements about values, methodology, or 
definitions that are driving the surface ones.

9. You are a skeptic. Read this research and find 
the weakest link in the chain of reasoning. 
The place where one challenged assumption makes 
the whole argument less convincing.

10. Read this and produce a one-page briefing 
written for a decision-maker who needs to act 
on it today. Include what they should do, 
what they should watch for, and what they 
should ignore.

11. Identify every claim in this document that 
is stated as fact but is actually an assumption. 
List each one with why it qualifies as an 
assumption rather than established fact.

12. Read this corpus and tell me what a journalist 
with no prior knowledge of this field would 
get wrong if they reported on it based on 
this material alone.

13. I'm trying to understand [TOPIC]. Read these 
sources and explain it to me the way a domain 
expert would explain it to an intelligent 
non-specialist. No jargon without definition.

14. Read everything and identify the three most 
counterintuitive findings. Things that contradict 
what most people in this space believe. 
Cite the specific source for each.

15. Produce a research brief that distinguishes 
between what we know, what we think we know, 
and what we are guessing. Label every claim 
with one of those three categories.

16. Read this and produce a devil's advocate 
brief. The strongest possible case against 
the position these sources collectively argue for.

17. What would change about the conclusions in 
this research if the most important data point 
were wrong? Walk me through the cascade.

18. Read these documents and identify every 
place where correlation is presented as 
causation. Cite each instance specifically.

19. Synthesize this research into a framework 
I can apply to similar situations in the future. 
Not a summary of what it says. A tool I can use.

20. Read this corpus and tell me what questions 
it raises that it doesn't answer. Rank them 
by importance to someone trying to make a 
decision based on this material.

21. I need to cite this research accurately. 
Read it and give me the five strongest claims 
that are well-supported enough to cite with 
confidence and five claims I should avoid 
citing because the support is weak.

22. Read everything and produce a gap analysis. 
What does someone who wants to understand 
this topic fully still need to research 
after reading all of this?

23. Treat this as a first draft of a research 
brief. Read it and tell me what a rigorous 
editor would push back on, demand evidence 
for, or cut entirely.

24. Read this corpus and identify the expert 
consensus, the minority position, and the 
fringe position. Be specific about where 
each lives in the source material.

25. I'm about to make a decision based on 
this research. Read it and tell me what 
I'm missing that I should know before deciding.

26. Read these documents and extract every 
specific data point with its source and date. 
Flag any data point that is more than 
three years old.

27. Synthesize this into a competitive 
intelligence brief. What does someone 
who reads this understand about the 
competitive landscape that someone who 
doesn't read it misses?

28. Read this and produce a timeline of 
how thinking on this topic has evolved 
based on publication dates of the sources.

29. What would Nassim Taleb say about 
the risk assessment in this research? 
Apply his framework to the claims made.

30. Read this corpus and produce the most 
honest one-paragraph summary possible. 
No spin. No framing. Just what the 
evidence actually shows.
Category 2: Writing and Content Production (30 Prompts)
31. Write this piece in my voice. Before drafting, 
tell me what you understand my voice to be based 
on the examples I've provided. Then write. 
Then tell me where you were uncertain.

32. I'm writing about [TOPIC]. Give me the angle 
that nobody in this space has taken that is 
also the most honest framing of what's 
actually happening.

33. Write a first draft. Then write a second draft 
that challenges every claim in the first draft. 
Then give me a third draft that incorporates 
what survives the challenge.

34. Take this rough outline and identify the 
section that is doing the most work carrying 
the argument. Develop that section fully 
before touching anything else.

35. Write the opening paragraph ten different ways. 
Each one should use a completely different 
approach to earning the reader's attention. 
Label each approach.

36. I have a piece that works but doesn't land. 
Read it and diagnose exactly what's preventing 
it from landing. Then rewrite the specific 
parts that are failing.

37. Write this article as if the reader is 
skeptical of the main claim from the first 
sentence. Every paragraph should earn 
the next one.

38. Take this draft and cut it by 40% without 
losing the argument. Then tell me what 
you cut and why each cut was right.

39. I need to write about [TOPIC] for an 
audience that thinks they already understand it. 
Write something that gives them genuine 
new understanding without making them 
feel patronized.

40. Write the version of this piece that would 
get the most pushback and the version that 
would get the most agreement. Then write 
the version that is most accurate regardless 
of reaction.

41. Take my rough notes on [TOPIC] and identify 
the strongest argument hiding in them. 
Build that argument into a complete piece.

42. Write three different introductions for 
this piece. One that leads with the problem. 
One that leads with the stakes. One that 
leads with a counterintuitive claim. 
Tell me which you would choose and why.

43. Read this draft and identify every sentence 
that is doing no work. Cut them. Then 
identify every sentence that is doing 
the wrong work and rewrite them.

44. Write this in the style of [PUBLICATION] 
without copying their tone. Match their 
standard for evidence and specificity 
while keeping my voice.

45. I need a conclusion that doesn't summarize. 
Write an ending that leaves the reader 
with something they didn't have before 
they started reading.

46. Take this piece and identify the single 
claim that the rest of the piece depends 
on. Make sure that claim is stated clearly, 
early, and supported better than any other.

47. Write the most useful version of this 
piece for someone who will read it once 
and needs to act on it immediately.

48. Read this and identify where I'm hedging 
unnecessarily. Rewrite those sections 
with the conviction the argument actually 
warrants.

49. Write a piece about [TOPIC] that would 
make someone who disagrees with me 
feel their position was understood 
before I made my case.

50. Take this outline and tell me which 
sections are load-bearing and which 
are padding. Cut the padding. 
Strengthen the load-bearing sections.

51. Write this piece backwards. Start with 
the conclusion and work backwards to 
the evidence. Sometimes that's the 
right structure.

52. I need to write about something 
complicated without oversimplifying it. 
Write the version that is genuinely 
accurate and still readable by 
a non-specialist.

53. Take my voice and this topic and write 
the piece I would write if I had spent 
three months researching it rather 
than three hours.

54. Write a draft. Then read it as 
a hostile editor and mark every 
place where a smart skeptic would 
stop reading. Fix those places.

55. I need a lede that makes this piece 
impossible to put down. Write five 
options. Each one should create a 
different type of tension.

56. Read this draft and tell me the one 
thing I'm afraid to say that I should 
say. Then write the version that says it.

57. Write the version of this piece that 
will be most shared. Not the most viral. 
The most genuinely useful to someone 
who shares it with someone they care about.

58. Take this topic and write the piece 
that will be cited as a reference 
five years from now. What makes 
something durable rather than timely?

59. Write this in under 500 words without 
losing the argument. Then write it 
in under 200 words without losing 
the core claim.

60. I have too much to say and not enough 
structure. Read my notes and build 
the architecture before writing a word.
Category 3: Business Strategy and Analysis (30 Prompts)
61. Read this business plan and tell me 
the three assumptions that, if wrong, 
make everything else irrelevant. 
Rank them by probability of being wrong.

62. I'm deciding between [OPTION A] and 
[OPTION B]. Give me the strongest 
case for each before giving me 
your recommendation. Then tell me 
what information would change 
your recommendation.

63. Analyze this market and tell me 
what a well-capitalized competitor 
who understood it deeply would 
do in the next 18 months.

64. Read this business and identify 
the single constraint limiting growth 
more than anything else. The one 
thing that, if removed, would 
change everything.

65. I need to pressure-test this strategy. 
Play the role of the smartest 
competitor in this market and 
tell me exactly how you would 
respond to what I'm planning.

66. Read this P&L and tell me what 
a CFO would find concerning that 
the founder is probably not worried 
enough about.

67. I'm building in [MARKET]. Tell me 
the three incumbents most threatened 
by what I'm building and how each 
would likely respond.

68. Analyze this company's positioning 
and tell me the gap they are 
leaving open that a well-positioned 
competitor could exploit.

69. Read this strategy document and 
identify every place where we 
are optimizing for a metric 
that doesn't actually predict 
what we care about.

70. I have a strategic decision to make. 
Walk me through the second and 
third-order effects of each option 
before I decide.

71. Read this competitive analysis 
and tell me what we got wrong 
about how our competitors think 
about their own business.

72. I need to prioritize. Read these 
ten initiatives and rank them by 
expected value times probability 
of success. Show your reasoning.

73. Analyze this market entry opportunity 
and tell me the three most likely 
reasons it fails within 24 months.

74. Read this and identify every place 
where we are confusing activity 
with progress. Where are we busy 
doing things that don't move the needle?

75. I need to make a build versus buy 
versus partner decision on [CAPABILITY]. 
Walk me through the full analysis 
before recommending.

76. Read this business and identify 
the single metric that predicts 
success better than any other. 
Explain why everything else 
is downstream of it.

77. I'm about to enter a pricing 
negotiation. Read this context 
and give me the five moves 
I should prepare for and 
how to respond to each.

78. Analyze this company's strengths 
and tell me which ones are 
actually defensible and which 
ones look like advantages 
but will erode.

79. I need to tell a story about 
this business that is true, 
compelling, and that our 
best customers immediately recognize 
as accurate. Write it.

80. Read this strategy and identify 
every assumption we are making 
about customer behavior that 
we haven't validated.

81. I'm three years into building this 
and I need fresh eyes. Read everything 
and tell me what you see that 
I probably can't see anymore 
because I'm too close to it.

82. Analyze this partnership opportunity 
and tell me the five ways it 
could go wrong that neither 
party is currently discussing.

83. I need to make this business 
more defensible. Read it and 
identify the three moats I 
could build that would be 
genuinely hard to replicate.

84. Read this growth strategy and 
tell me what we're assuming about 
retention that our acquisition 
numbers might be hiding.

85. I'm building a board presentation. 
Read this material and tell me 
the three questions a good board 
member will ask that we are 
not currently prepared to answer.

86. Analyze this market and tell me 
who the real customer is versus 
who we think the customer is.

87. Read this and identify where 
we have a feature versus where 
we have a product versus where 
we have a business.

88. I need to allocate budget across 
these initiatives. Read them and 
tell me where the highest leverage 
dollar is and where we are 
most likely wasting money.

89. Analyze this competitive response 
and tell me whether it reflects 
fear or strategy. The distinction 
matters for how we respond.

90. Read this business model and 
identify the unit economics assumption 
that most needs to be validated 
before we scale.
Category 4: Code and Technical Work (30 Prompts)
91. Read this entire codebase and 
tell me the three architectural 
decisions that will be most painful 
to reverse as we scale.

92. I need to refactor this. Before 
touching anything, map the 
dependencies and tell me the 
order that minimizes risk.

93. Read this code and identify 
every place where we have 
technical debt that is actively 
slowing down new development.

94. Write this function then review 
it as a senior engineer who 
cares about edge cases. 
Then fix what the review finds.

95. I need to debug this. Walk me 
through your reasoning as you 
diagnose it rather than just 
giving me the answer.

96. Read this codebase and write 
documentation that would let 
a new engineer be productive 
in their first week.

97. Review this code for security 
vulnerabilities. For each one 
found, explain the attack vector 
and the fix.

98. I need to optimize this for 
performance. Profile it first, 
identify the bottleneck, then 
optimize only what matters.

99. Write tests for this code. 
Focus on the edge cases that 
could cause production failures, 
not the happy path.

100. Read this architecture and tell 
me what breaks first when 
traffic is ten times current volume.

101. I'm starting a new project. 
Read my requirements and 
recommend the technology stack 
with honest tradeoffs for each choice.

102. Review this pull request as 
a senior engineer who cares 
about maintainability above 
cleverness.

103. Read this code and tell me 
what the original developer 
was thinking, where they 
made assumptions, and where 
those assumptions might be wrong.

104. I need to migrate this to 
[NEW TECHNOLOGY]. Read the 
current implementation and 
produce a migration plan 
that minimizes downtime.

105. Write this API. Then write 
the documentation. Then write 
the tests. In that order.

106. Read this error log and tell 
me what's happening in plain 
English before giving me the fix.

107. I need code review. Read 
this and tell me everything 
a senior engineer would say 
in a code review. Don't hold back.

108. Build this feature. Before writing 
code, tell me the design decision 
you're making and why. 
Then implement it.

109. Read this codebase and identify 
the functions that are doing 
more than one thing. Split them.

110. I need to handle this edge case. 
Read the surrounding code and 
implement the fix in a way 
that fits the existing patterns.

111. Write the simplest possible 
implementation of this feature. 
Then tell me what I'd need 
to add to make it production-ready.

112. Read this code and tell me 
where we are relying on 
undefined behavior or 
implementation details 
that could change.

113. I'm doing a security audit. 
Read this codebase and 
prioritize the vulnerabilities 
by severity and exploitability.

114. Write this in the pattern 
that will be easiest to 
modify in six months when 
requirements change.

115. Read this and tell me 
everywhere we have a 
magic number that should 
be a named constant.

116. I need to scale this database 
query. Read the schema and 
the query, explain why it's 
slow, and fix it.

117. Review this for accessibility. 
Tell me what a user with 
[SPECIFIC LIMITATION] would 
experience and fix the issues.

118. Read this code and write 
a runbook for the on-call 
engineer when this service fails.

119. I need to implement [FEATURE]. 
Read the existing codebase 
first and implement it in a 
way that fits the existing 
architecture rather than 
introducing new patterns.

120. Read this and tell me 
which parts I should delete 
rather than maintain.
Category 5: Decision Support (30 Prompts)
121. I'm deciding [DECISION]. Give me 
the frame I'm not seeing because 
I'm too close to it.

122. Walk me through the pre-mortem 
for this decision. It's two years 
from now and it failed. 
What happened?

123. I've made this decision emotionally. 
Now give me the rational case 
for the opposite choice so I 
can decide properly.

124. What am I optimizing for with 
this decision that I haven't 
stated explicitly? Name it 
and tell me if that's actually 
what I should be optimizing for.

125. I have all the information I 
think I need to decide. 
What am I missing that I 
don't know I'm missing?

126. Give me the decision tree 
for this choice. Every branch. 
Every node. Every probability 
I should assign.

127. I'm about to make a 
reversible decision as if 
it's irreversible. Tell me 
what that costs me.

128. I'm about to make an 
irreversible decision as if 
it's reversible. Tell me 
what that costs me.

129. What would I decide if 
I knew I had to live with 
the consequences for ten years?

130. Tell me the decision that 
I'm avoiding making by 
making this one.

131. I need to make this decision 
with incomplete information. 
What's the minimum I need 
to know to decide responsibly?

132. Give me the base rate 
for this type of decision. 
How often do people who 
make this choice in these 
circumstances get the outcome 
they're hoping for?

133. I think the risk here is [X]. 
What risk am I underweighting?

134. Walk me through the second 
and third-order effects of 
this decision that I'm 
probably not thinking about.

135. What does this decision 
signal to the people 
watching me make it?

136. I'm deciding between fast 
and right. Help me figure 
out which one actually 
matters more here.

137. What would I need to believe 
for the option I'm leaning 
against to be the right choice?

138. Give me the version of 
this decision that I would 
be proud to explain to 
someone I respect.

139. I've been going back and 
forth on this. What does 
that tell you about what 
I actually value?

140. What's the cost of not 
deciding right now?

141. I'm treating this as a 
binary choice. What's the 
third option I'm missing?

142. Give me the decision that 
optimizes for optionality 
rather than outcome.

143. What would a person who 
has made this decision 
successfully ten times 
know that I don't?

144. I need to make this 
decision without being 
able to explain it fully. 
What's the simplest 
justification that's 
also honest?

145. Walk me through how 
I'll feel about this 
decision in a week, 
a month, and a year.

146. What's the asymmetry here? 
How bad is the downside 
versus how good is the upside?

147. I'm deciding this under 
time pressure. Tell me 
if the time pressure 
is real or manufactured.

148. What would the person 
I want to become decide?

149. Give me one reason 
to decide now and one 
reason to wait. 
Then tell me which 
one is right.

150. What am I afraid 
of that I haven't said?
Category 6: Communication and Persuasion (30 Prompts)
151. I need to persuade [PERSON] 
to [ACTION]. Write the message 
that starts from where they 
are rather than where I want 
them to be.

152. Read this email and tell me 
how the recipient will feel 
after reading the first sentence, 
the third paragraph, and the close.

153. I need to say something difficult. 
Write the version that is honest 
without being unkind and direct 
without being brutal.

154. Write this message for someone 
who reads fast and decides 
in the first ten seconds.

155. I need to decline this 
without damaging the relationship. 
Write the no that keeps the door open.

156. Read this message I received 
and tell me what they're 
actually asking for beneath 
what they said.

157. I need to give feedback 
that will be heard rather 
than defended against. 
Write it so the other 
person can receive it.

158. Write the follow-up message 
for someone who hasn't 
responded in two weeks 
that doesn't sound desperate.

159. I need to make a request 
of someone more senior than me. 
Write the ask that respects 
their time and makes yes easy.

160. Read this message and tell 
me every assumption I'm making 
about how the recipient 
will interpret it.

161. I need to communicate bad 
news. Write the message 
that is honest about what 
happened and clear about 
what happens next.

162. Write this pitch for someone 
who has heard a hundred 
pitches this week and is 
actively looking for reasons to say no.

163. I need to escalate this issue 
without damaging my relationship 
with the person I'm escalating about.

164. Write the message that makes 
this complex thing simple 
without making it simplistic.

165. I need to apologize in a way 
that repairs rather than 
just acknowledges. Write it.

166. Read this thread and tell 
me where the miscommunication 
started and how to repair it.

167. Write the version of this 
proposal that makes the 
reader feel like they came 
up with the idea.

168. I need to say no to a 
good opportunity for the 
right reasons. Write the 
response that explains 
without over-explaining.

169. Write this announcement 
for the person who will 
be most skeptical of it.

170. I need to negotiate this. 
Write the opening position 
that gives me room to move 
while establishing what I 
actually need.

171. Read this and tell me 
the subtext. What is 
this person actually saying?

172. Write the response to 
this criticism that is 
neither defensive nor 
dismissive.

173. I need to align two people 
who disagree. Write the 
message that finds the 
common ground they don't 
know they share.

174. Write this update for 
an audience that includes 
people who will use anything 
negative to undermine the project.

175. I need to communicate 
uncertainty without losing 
confidence. Write the message 
that is honest about what 
we don't know while clear 
about what we do.

176. Write the version of 
this message that the 
recipient will forward 
to someone else because 
it's that useful.

177. I need to set a boundary 
professionally. Write it 
clearly without making 
it an ultimatum.

178. Read this and tell me 
what I should ask before 
I respond.

179. Write this cold outreach 
for someone who deletes 
cold outreach immediately.

180. I need to close this. 
Write the message that 
makes it easy for them 
to say yes right now.
Category 7: Learning and Knowledge Development (30 Prompts)
181. Explain [CONCEPT] to me 
the way a world-class 
teacher would explain it 
to someone who is intelligent 
but has no background in this field.

182. I think I understand [CONCEPT]. 
Test me. Ask me questions 
that reveal whether I 
actually understand it 
or just know the vocabulary.

183. What's the thing about 
[TOPIC] that most people 
get wrong because they 
learned the simplified version?

184. Give me the mental model 
for [DOMAIN] that an expert 
uses that a beginner doesn't have.

185. I've read a lot about 
[TOPIC]. What should I 
read next that will 
genuinely change how I think 
rather than just add more 
of what I already know?

186. Explain this to me using 
an analogy from [COMPLETELY DIFFERENT DOMAIN]. 
Make the analogy earn its place.

187. What are the three questions 
about [TOPIC] that separate 
people who really understand it 
from people who just know about it?

188. I'm learning [SKILL]. 
What's the deliberate practice 
that produces the most 
improvement fastest?

189. Teach me the history of 
[IDEA] in a way that helps 
me understand why it took 
so long to figure out.

190. What would change about 
how I approach [TOPIC] if 
I understood it the way 
a genuine expert does?

191. Give me the curriculum 
for becoming genuinely 
good at [SKILL] in 
six months if I can 
dedicate two hours per day.

192. What's the thing about 
[FIELD] that took you 
the longest to understand 
and that everyone new 
to the field struggles with?

193. Explain the distinction 
between [CONCEPT A] and 
[CONCEPT B] in a way 
that makes it impossible 
to confuse them again.

194. What's the best argument 
against the thing I 
just told you I believe? 
Make it as strong as you can.

195. I need to learn enough 
about [TOPIC] to have 
a credible conversation 
with an expert. 
What do I need to know?

196. Give me the Feynman 
explanation of [CONCEPT]. 
Simple enough to explain 
to a ten-year-old without 
being wrong.

197. What are the five books 
that have most shaped 
thinking in [FIELD] and 
what's the core idea 
from each?

198. I understand the what 
of [TOPIC]. Help me 
understand the why 
at a deeper level.

199. What's the question about 
[TOPIC] that I should 
be asking but haven't 
thought to ask yet?

200. Teach me [CONCEPT] by 
starting with the problem 
it was invented to solve.

201. What's the thing about 
[FIELD] that is widely 
believed but wrong?

202. I need to build intuition 
for [DOMAIN] not just 
knowledge. What's 
the difference and 
how do I get it?

203. Give me the three 
most important experiments 
or case studies that 
shaped our understanding 
of [TOPIC].

204. What would I believe 
differently about [TOPIC] 
if I were an expert 
in it versus where 
I am now?

205. I've hit a learning 
plateau with [SKILL]. 
Diagnose why and 
tell me how to break through.

206. Teach me this concept 
by showing me where 
it breaks down. 
The edge cases reveal 
more than the core.

207. What's the vocabulary 
I need to understand 
to think clearly about 
[TOPIC] that I'm currently missing?

208. I want to learn from 
the best practitioners 
in [FIELD]. What do 
they do differently 
from everyone else?

209. Give me the diagnostic 
questions that reveal 
whether I understand 
[CONCEPT] or just 
think I understand it.

210. Teach me [TOPIC] in 
a way that will be 
wrong in five years 
versus in a way 
that will still be right.
Category 8: Productivity and Systems (30 Prompts)
211. Read my project list and 
tell me which one I'm avoiding 
and what that avoidance is costing me.

212. I'm overwhelmed. Read 
everything on my plate and 
tell me what would happen 
if I did nothing on each item. 
That determines the real priority.

213. Design a weekly structure 
for someone who does 
[TYPE OF WORK] that 
protects the highest-value 
work from being crowded out 
by urgent but unimportant tasks.

214. I have three hours of 
focused time. Tell me 
exactly how to use it 
given these constraints 
and objectives.

215. Read my workflow and 
identify every handoff 
that introduces friction 
or delays without adding value.

216. I keep not doing [TASK]. 
Diagnose why and give me 
a system that removes 
whatever is blocking me.

217. Design a capture system 
for someone whose best 
ideas come at the worst times.

218. I'm in too many meetings. 
Read this calendar and 
tell me which ones I 
should stop attending, 
which I should delegate, 
and which I should redesign.

How to Use These Prompts With Fable 5
The prompts above work at a different level with Fable 5 than with previous models for three specific reasons.
The context window changes what's possible.
Prompts in categories one and four feed entire codebases, research corpora, and document libraries as context. Previous models could not hold enough context to make those prompts work at full depth. Fable 5's million token window means the prompt and the full context fit in a single session.
The honesty rate changes what's reliable.
Prompts that ask Claude to challenge your assumptions, identify what you're missing, or tell you what you need to hear rather than what you want to hear depend on the model being willing to be honest. Fable 5's honesty rate dropped from 65% to under 5% for self-reporting failures. The prompts asking for genuine challenge rather than polished validation now work more reliably.
The autonomous operation capability changes what's sustainable.
The most powerful use of these prompts is not running them manually one at a time. It is encoding the most valuable ones as skills in Hermes Agent and having them run automatically against your vault, your projects, and your accumulated context on a schedule.
Prompt 1 becomes a weekly research synthesis skill. Prompt 121 becomes a decision support skill called before any significant decision. Prompt 211 becomes a Monday morning priority review.
At that point the 300 prompts are not tools you use.
They are infrastructure that runs your thinking for you.
That is what Fable 5 was built for.
Start with the five prompts in each category that are most relevant to your current work. Run them this week. Identify the three that produce the most valuable outputs. Encode those three as skills.
Build from there.
Follow @cyrilXBT for every Fable 5 prompt architecture, skill template, and autonomous workflow that makes this model work at its full capability level.","cover_media":"3_2065825317340479488"}
